the final colour may be darker if starting with yellow besan
cooking on low heat lets you stop stirring often but 1 might be too low
even after this much water, mom was right and the water can be fixed by cooking longer

variations.name: steamed

steps.action: proof in a bowl with floured liner cloth

notes: basically the game is to have high hydration cooked in steam most of the time

variations.name: long-specific

steps.action: bake just below middle rack on tawa

notes: guided from the french bakery from the claudia insider foods video
first attempt was terrible at 75% hydration and it didn't rise much
splitting into two was better and helped the rise but the crust was still too hard
20 minutes was enough without an ice cube but might need 25 with steam
tried with a normal sheet tray covered with an upside down deep baking tray which did work okay

variations.name: dry
notes: relatively dense even after kneading
hydration with whole wheat to get best rise but still workable dough is tricky but 80%+ gets really wet
67% hydration was easy to handle but the bread was fairly dense
80% hydration is right at the edge of being able to handle with difficulty
foccacia(sorta) at 120% hydration is great but unworkable
overproofing is really easy to do with high hydration doughs

variations.name: flax
notes: 45 minutes is perhaps a little long but it's okay
420 ml water seemed okay but trying more

variations.name: normal
notes: best with warm rice and cold milk

variations.name: normal

steps.action: knead with dough hook on low speed
cold ferment in the fridge overnight
wet skin of the dough with fingertips if it's very dry
score using serrated knife if needed

notes: white flour is noticeably smoother than whole wheat
using the paddle attachment did something wrong with the dough making it tear apart

adding water to the dough mid knead does work but doesn't look any different than just adding it at the beginning but the guy on youtube (Una pizza) does it so I guess I will too. actually coming back later, it might be really important cause i'm having trouble explaining why the pizza today was so good
putting water on top of the dough then putting cloth makes the dough stick to the cloth
two days cold ferment didn't really do extra but may have resulted in worse proofing, perhaps because of moisture loss? nope, it was because of overkneading
proofing before cold ferment also didn't do much and seriouseats also did a proof after a cold ferment
use as little flour as possible when shaping the dough
leaving outside at room temperature too long will dry out the dough
putting too much water on the surface of the dough may make it really sticky
proofing inside the bowl made the texture overly bready but proofing outside didn't do much better
kneading and adding water just until the dough comes together works well
for large batches, just split into four after keeping it in the fridge
don't use parchment paper for raw dough
mold somehow developed after i split the balls, floured the bottom, and proofed in a baking tray. unsure which one it was or all three
separating salt from the instant yeast doesn't seem to matter

variations.name: boiled

steps.action: place eggs in cold water in freezer till cool
crack lightly on counter and peel

notes: SeriousEats kenji's instructions helped as a really great starting point
8 for soft yolk, 9 and a half for firm yolk

steps.action: fry with lid until whites are solid

variations.name: normal

substeps.action: put flour on bottom of crust
spread crust out from the center keeping a ridge
pull on peel right before placing in oven
rotate pizza and watch at the top of the last minute

notes: rub some flour on the peel as well
keeping the dough on the peel for too long makes it stick because of moisture underneath
not putting enough flour on the bottom of the crust makes it stick to the peel
seven minutes was necessary in the corner but six is often good enough in the center of the oven
lowered to six minutes for normal pizza for 180 g crust

waiting two minutes to reheat tawa was too little for a personal pizza
rotate the pizza 180 degrees halfway through broiling
put the toppings on when the parchment paper is taken off so they don't overcook or dry out
